generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotor : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis with closed-loop control capabilities, constrained by a safety cell limiting range to [-90°, 90°]. Enables real-time trajectory planning and adaptive control strategies for dynamic motion coordination."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingProcessEnvironment
]

instance SafetyCell : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Hardware-level safety mechanism enforcing maximum working range limits [-90°, 90°] for the robotic drive to prevent operational errors."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingProcessEnvironment
]

instance SiemensSinumerikControllerInterface : DTDFVocab:Interface [
    base:desc "MQTT-based remote interface for command transmission supporting distributed control of the robotic drives with real-time trajectory planning capabilities."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingProcessEnvironment
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance DrivePositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-resolution position encoder providing real-time kinematic tracking with path repeatability of 0.06 mm and positional accuracy of 0.1 mm. Sampled at ≥1 kHz with hardware-level noise filtering."
    DTDFVocab:producedData DrivePositionDataTransmitted
    base:isContainedIn RoboticDriveAssembly
]

instance DrivePositionEncoder2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Second high-resolution position encoder per drive with identical specifications to DrivePositionEncoder, providing redundant kinematic tracking for fault tolerance."
    DTDFVocab:producedData DrivePositionDataTransmitted
    base:isContainedIn RoboticDriveAssembly
]

instance RoboticDriveAssembly : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Spatial assembly containing two position encoders per drive axis enabling real-time kinematic tracking through distributed sensor placement."
    base:contains DrivePositionEncoder, DrivePositionEncoder2
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingProcessEnvironment
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance DrivePositionDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom DrivePositionEncoder, DrivePositionEncoder2
]

instance MQTTProtocol : DTDFVocab:CommunicationProtocol [
    base:desc "Message Queuing Telemetry Transport protocol enabling low-latency streaming of sensor data from physical system to digital twin with lightweight communication efficiency."
]

instance HardwareNoiseFiltering : DTDFVocab:DataQualityAssurance [
    base:desc "Hardware-level filtering mechanism applied at sensor level to mitigate noise and ensure data integrity for control and modeling purposes."
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance KinematicModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based white-box model implemented in MathWorks Simulink for dynamic simulation of robotic drive kinematics, incorporating friction modeling and nonlinear behavior analysis."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DecisionSupportEnabler
]

instance SysMLInternalBlockDiagram : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML IBD representing functional architecture of robotic drive system with components, interfaces, and connectors for traceability between physical and digital representations."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ModelBasedSystemsEngineeringToolchain
]

instance SimulinkBehavioralModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model implemented in MathWorks Simulink for detailed simulation of drive dynamics, including adaptive control strategies and hybrid modeling approaches."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ModelBasedSystemsEngineeringToolchain
]

instance ModelBasedSystemsEngineeringToolchain : DTDFVocab:Toolchain [
    base:desc "Integration framework combining SysML, Simulink, and general-purpose languages (C++, Java) for MBSE methodology implementation across abstraction layers."
]

instance DrivePositionData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated and abstracted positional data traces derived from DrivePositionDataTransmitted, serving as input for kinematic model calibration and behavior simulation."
    DTDFVocab:fromData DrivePositionDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo KinematicModel, SysMLInternalBlockDiagram
]

instance ContextualDataAggregator : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processing component aggregating and abstracting raw sensor data traces into meaningful operational metrics for decision support systems."
    DTDFVocab:fromData DrivePositionDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DecisionSupportEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance DecisionSupportEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Software component implementing processing logic for real-time or near-real-time analytics including predictive maintenance and performance optimization based on aggregated data traces."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, OptimizationService, PredictionService
]

instance ModelBasedSystemsEngineeringEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MBSE methodology enabler supporting traceability and version control across requirements, functional, logical, and physical layers through SPES/SMArDT frameworks."
    DTDFVocab:enables ModelConcretizationService
]

instance ModelConcretizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service transforming abstract reference models into conforming concrete models through incarnation of reference concepts, ensuring alignment between physical and digital representations."
]

// C6: Services
instance MonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time data aggregation and abstraction service providing contextual insights for process health monitoring through vibration, torque, and positional data analysis."
    DTDFVocab:provides ProcessHealthInsight
]

instance OptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Model-based optimization service adjusting physical parameters (friction, wear) using digital shadow data traces to improve manufacturing process efficiency and reduce downtime."
    DTDFVocab:provides ProcessOptimizationAction
]

instance PredictionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Prognostic analytics service forecasting equipment degradation and process deviations based on historical and real-time data traces for predictive maintenance."
    DTDFVocab:provides DegradationForecastInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance ProcessHealthInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Contextual insight derived from aggregated sensor data providing real-time assessment of manufacturing process health and operational status."
]

instance ProcessOptimizationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated adjustment of manufacturing parameters to optimize process efficiency based on model predictions and optimization algorithms."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance DegradationForecastInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predictive insight forecasting potential equipment failures and process deviations based on analyzed data patterns and historical trends."
]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingProcessEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Industry 4.0 manufacturing environment focusing on continuous production systems with high-precision requirements for injection molding and similar processes."
    base:contains PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotor, SafetyCell, RoboticDriveAssembly, SiemensSinumerikControllerInterface
]

instance DigitalTwinEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Digital environment hosting the Digital Twin with SysML-based architecture models and Simulink behavioral simulations."
    base:contains SysMLInternalBlockDiagram, SimulinkBehavioralModel, DecisionSupportEnabler, KinematicModel, ModelBasedSystemsEngineeringToolchain, ContextualDataAggregator, DrivePositionData
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the described Digital Twin system is **not explicitly detailed** in the provided documents. However, the distinction between **digital shadows** (passive, one-way data flow) and **Digital Twins** (active, bidirectional) [32] implies that a DT could include:

- **Control Flows**: Hypothetical **command types** (e.g., parameter adjustments, maintenance triggers) sent from the DT to physical systems, though no specific formats (e.g., REST APIs, OPC UA) or protocols are mentioned.
- **Validation/Authentication**: No explicit mechanisms described, but **security protocols** (e.g., TLS, OAuth) for network communication (see `dt_technical_connection`) may apply to control messages.
- **Feedback Systems**: Not addressed; the focus is on **digital shadows** as passive entities, not interactive control loops.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Not mentioned; assumes passive data flow or external systems handle conflicts (e.g., via middleware like ThingsBoard [53]).
- **Command Execution Confirmation**: No details provided; likely relies on **acknowledgment mechanisms** in the physical system’s communication layer (e.g., MQTT QoS levels).

**Note**: Since the documents emphasize **digital shadows** (one-way data) and do not discuss active DT control, this field is **inferred from conceptual distinctions** rather than direct evidence. For concrete implementations, refer to broader DT literature (e.g., [35]–[37]).
"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's temporal aspects are governed by the **one-way data flow** between physical and digital components, as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. While explicit synchronization frequencies or latency requirements are not detailed, the model implies **real-time or near-real-time processing** of data traces (e.g., sensor inputs, operational logs) to maintain consistency with the physical system. The **temporal granularity** is determined by the **data trace collection frequency**, which must align with the **processing component’s computational steps** (e.g., simulation or data calculation models) to ensure accurate aggregation and abstraction. The **transitive traceability** of data traces [2] further enforces temporal coherence, as computed traces inherit the granularity of their input sources. No specific time scales (e.g., millisecond, second-level) or service-level latency targets (e.g., <100ms) are mentioned in the documents."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **orchestration and coordination** of the Digital Twin system is framed within the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** [32], which emphasizes a **processing-centric architecture** where a **Processing component** consumes input data traces and produces output data via **data calculation or simulation models** [2]. The **architecture patterns** are derived from **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies [17], which integrate **architecture description languages (ADLs)** (e.g., SysML, AADL) to define components, interfaces, and interconnections. The **resource management** is implicitly handled by the **traceability framework**, which ensures that data traces are linked to their processing logic (e.g., models, simulation tools like Simulink [28]). System-wide coordination is achieved through the **conforming model’s conformance relation** [41], where the **concretization** of the reference model maps abstract concepts (e.g., data traces, processing components) to concrete elements. No explicit mention of **orchestration frameworks** (e.g., Kubernetes, microservices) or **real-time coordination protocols** (e.g., DDS, MQTT) is provided."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system, as implied by the provided documents, is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, which decompose system modeling into hierarchical abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle transitions between these stages involve **concrete concretizations of reference models**, where a **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (e.g., UML class diagrams) is instantiated into a **conforming model** tailored to specific use cases (e.g., injection molding). Transition management is governed by **traceability mechanisms**—such as transitive data traceability from processing components to input/output data traces—ensuring alignment between digital and physical system states. Lifecycle governance is implicitly supported by **purpose-driven design** (e.g., starting from data accessibility or requirements) and **methodological frameworks** like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) or OOSEM, which guide iterative refinement of models across stages. Metadata aggregation and abstraction further enable dynamic adaptation of the DT’s representation throughout its lifecycle."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
Model fidelity is ensured through a **multi-layered abstraction hierarchy** spanning requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers, with **architecture description languages (ADLs)** and **SysML-based modeling** validating structural consistency. Behavioral fidelity relies on **hybrid white-box/black-box modeling**, combining **analytical simulations** (e.g., Simulink) with **data-driven abstractions** to capture nonlinear effects like friction. Validation and verification (V&V) processes include **traceability checks** between MBSE models and simulation tools, ensuring correctness of transformations. Uncertainty quantification is addressed via **sensor precision specifications (0.06 mm repeatability, 0.1 mm accuracy)** and **model calibration against real-time data streams**. Error detection mechanisms leverage **closed-loop control feedback** and **MQTT-based data integrity checks**, while quality assurance is reinforced through **safety cell constraints** and **hardware-level actuation limits**. Probabilistic or adaptive methods (e.g., latent variable models) are **not referenced** in the provided documents.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The technical connection for this Digital Twin system is primarily defined by the **MQTT protocol** for sensor data transmission from physical objects to the digital representation, as mentioned in the context. No further details regarding network topology, bandwidth requirements, latency specifications, or redundancy mechanisms are provided in the documents. The system does not explicitly describe bidirectional communication or control flows, focusing instead on the **passive aggregation of data traces** for digital shadows. Security protocols or authentication mechanisms are not mentioned in the provided text."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system adheres to **industry standards** including:
  - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for architecture and behavior modeling (e.g., IBDs, state machines).
  - **UML (Unified Modeling Language)** for reference model definitions (e.g., Digital Shadow Reference Model as a UML Class Diagram).
  - **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework for **interoperability**, as explicitly referenced in the Abstract and Index Terms ('implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'). Compliance is ensured via **conformance relations** (e.g., mapping AAS concepts to concrete Digital Twin components) and **interoperability standards** (e.g., AAS Information Model for semantic consistency). Certification requirements are implied through adherence to **methodological frameworks** (e.g., SMArDT, SPES) that standardize development processes."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system incorporates safety mechanisms aligned with industrial automation standards, including a **safety cell** for the robotic system (e.g., MABI robot) that restricts the maximum working range to predefined boundaries, ensuring physical safety during operation. Data transmission is facilitated via **MQTT protocol** (as referenced in [52]), enabling real-time streaming of sensor and operational data between the physical asset and its digital counterpart. However, explicit details regarding cybersecurity protocols (e.g., encryption standards, authentication mechanisms like OAuth 2.0 or TLS), access control frameworks (e.g., role-based access control), or compliance with cybersecurity regulations (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001) are not provided in the documents. Similarly, fail-safe mechanisms beyond the physical safety cell (e.g., emergency stop integration, redundant data validation) are not described. Risk assessment approaches or safety-critical system considerations (e.g., functional safety per IEC 61508 or ISO 13849) are also not mentioned in the provided context."
]
